draft.introduction: John 11 narrates the raising of Lazarus, the climactic sign in the Fourth Gospel that precipitates the plot to kill Jesus. It contains the declaration “I am the resurrection and the life” and the shortest verse in the Bible: “Jesus wept.”

translation.text: Jesus said to her, ‘I am the resurrection and the life. Whoever believes in me, though he die, yet shall he live, and everyone who lives and believes in me shall never die. Do you believe this?’ She said to him, ‘Yes, Lord; I believe that you are the Christ, the Son of God.’
translation.original_text: εἶπεν αὐτῇ ὁ Ἰησοῦς · ἐγώ εἰμι ἡ ἀνάστασις καὶ ἡ ζωή

translation.text: When he had said these things, he cried out with a loud voice, ‘Lazarus, come out.’ The man who had died came out, his hands and feet bound with linen strips, and his face wrapped with a cloth. Jesus said to them, ‘Unbind him, and let him go.’
translation.original_text: ταῦτα εἰπὼν φωνῇ μεγάλῃ ἐκραύγασεν · Λάζαρε δεῦρο ἔξω

translator_notes.note: This is the fifth of the seven “I am” sayings in John. Martha’s confession that follows is the Johannine equivalent of Peter’s confession in the Synoptics, though here it is a woman who makes the definitive christological statement.
